content.text: Complex tasks that exceed what a single LLM prompt can reliably accomplish can be broken into a sequence of smaller, specialised prompts — each with a clear input, a specific responsibility, and a validated output that feeds the next stage. This is prompt chaining, and the sequential form is its simplest and most predictable variant.

A sequential prompt chain is a fixed-order pipeline in which the output of each prompt stage becomes the input to the next. Unlike autonomous agent loops where the model decides which tool to call or which stage to visit, a sequential chain has a predetermined structure: Stage 1 runs, produces output_1; Stage 2 receives output_1 as input and produces output_2; and so on until the final stage produces the result. There is no branching based on model output, no self-directed tool selection, and no loop — only a linear flow from input to output through a known sequence of steps.
The canonical sequential pattern breaks a complex task into three phases:
(enriching, scoring, or routing the structured data), and
(producing a final response informed by the earlier stages). Each stage can use a different model, different temperature settings, and a different system prompt optimised for its specific sub-task.

A single large prompt that asks an LLM to simultaneously extract entities, classify intent, verify constraints, and generate a response is asking the model to hold too many concerns in context at once. The result is typically inferior to breaking the task into stages: the model at each stage has a shorter, clearer instruction set, smaller context, and a more specific objective. Quality improves because the model's attention is focused; cost may improve because earlier stages can use smaller, cheaper models that are specialised for the sub-task.

Sequential chaining also dramatically improves debuggability. When a monolithic prompt fails, the failure could originate anywhere in the complex instruction set. When a sequential chain fails, the point of failure is precisely localised to whichever stage produced invalid output — identifiable from the intermediate output logs.
A model that is asked to do one thing clearly does it better than a model asked to do five things simultaneously. Specialised stages produce higher-quality intermediate results.
Intermediate outputs are inspectable. When something goes wrong, you can pinpoint which stage introduced the error.
Use a fast, cheap model for extraction; a specialised model for classification; a powerful model for generation. Total cost and latency can be lower than using one large model for all stages.
Each stage can be tested independently with unit-test-style input-output pairs, enabling safe iteration without end-to-end testing on every change.
Individual stage prompts can be updated without breaking the entire pipeline, as long as the output schema of the modified stage is preserved.
The execution order is fixed and inspectable in code, not decided by the model at runtime. Debugging and reasoning about the system is straightforward.

Each stage only receives the information it needs, keeping prompts short and avoiding context window pressure. The raw document is not passed to the generation stage; only the structured extraction is.
Expensive extraction stages can be cached per document. Downstream generation stages can be re-run with different prompts without re-running extraction.
A failure at Stage 2 stops the pipeline before Stage 3 is invoked, preventing wasted computation and preventing corrupted intermediate data from propagating.

Different stages can use the best model for their sub-task — a vision model for image description, a code model for code generation, a language model for prose synthesis — within a single coherent pipeline.

An error in an early stage propagates to all subsequent stages. Stage 1 extracting the wrong date means Stage 2 classifies based on the wrong date and Stage 3 generates a response about the wrong date. Error propagation is the primary risk of sequential chaining.

Each stage adds sequential latency. A 5-stage chain with 500ms per stage has minimum 2.5 second latency even with perfect caching. Parallelism is not available within a sequential chain by definition.
The chain cannot adapt its structure based on what it discovers. A chain that always runs 5 stages will run 5 stages even when the input could be handled correctly in 2.
The output schema of each stage is the interface between stages. Schema changes to Stage N require changes to the Stage N+1 prompt that references those fields.
Tasks that require dynamic tool selection, backtracking, or conditional execution of different stage sequences require an agent loop, not a sequential chain.

Each stage in a sequential chain is an independent LLM call with its own system prompt, model configuration, and output schema. The stage receives the accumulated context — the original input plus the outputs of all previous stages — and produces one new structured output. The key discipline is keeping the stage's context window as small as possible: pass only what the stage needs, not the full history.

The interface between stages is a typed handoff: Stage N produces output_N, and Stage N+1's system prompt explicitly references the fields of output_N by name. This contract must be documented and versioned. When Stage N's schema changes, Stage N+1's prompt must be updated to match. Treating the inter-stage interface as a typed API — not a free-form text string — is the single most important discipline for maintaining a sequential chain over time.
Stage N's output schema is defined as a Pydantic model in shared code.
Stage N's system prompt includes the output schema as part of the tool definition.
Stage N+1's system prompt explicitly names the fields it will receive and describes what it must do with each.
The pipeline runner validates output_N against the Pydantic model before passing it to Stage N+1.
As the chain progresses, the pipeline runner composes the context for each stage. There are two composition strategies:
(pass all previous outputs to each stage) and

(pass only the immediately relevant data). Full history preserves maximum context but inflates prompt size and cost. Selective handoff is cheaper but requires the pipeline designer to explicitly decide what each stage needs — a decision that documents the stage's dependencies and prevents implicit coupling between non-adjacent stages.

The diagram below shows a three-stage sequential chain. Each stage has its own system prompt, model, and validated output. The pipeline runner validates output at each handoff before passing it to the next stage.

attrs.alt: Sequential prompt chain: Raw Input goes to Stage 1 (extract/parse, fast model, temp=0, schema, validated output), then Stage 2 (classify/score, specialized model, validated output), then Stage 3 (generate/respond, capable model) to produce final output. Key properties: stage isolation, validated handoff, short context windows, error containment.

content.text: When to Use Sequential Prompt Chaining
Sequential Chains Are the Right Tool When
The task decomposes naturally into steps where each step's output is clearly defined and typed.
Different steps benefit from different model capabilities (e.g., extraction vs. generation).
The pipeline needs to be debuggable, testable, and maintainable by a team.
Early steps are expensive and benefit from caching independently of later steps.
The execution order is always the same regardless of the input content.
Choose an Agent Loop Instead When
The number of steps, the order of steps, or which tools to invoke depends on what the model discovers during execution.
The task requires backtracking — re-running an earlier step with new information.
The task has an open-ended search component where the model needs to decide when to stop.
Human approval is needed at intermediate points that cannot be predetermined.
Identify the natural sub-tasks. Good stage boundaries are points where the output can be expressed as a complete, typed data structure — not a partially-formed answer.
Write Pydantic models for the output of each stage before writing any prompts. These schemas are the pipeline's type system.
Each stage's system prompt should be fully specified and testable in isolation. It should not assume anything about stages it does not directly receive output from.
Write code that calls stages in order, validates each output against its schema, and passes the validated output to the next stage. The runner should log intermediate outputs.
Use Pydantic validation at every stage boundary. A validation failure at Stage 2 is far cheaper to debug than a corrupted output that surfaces at Stage 5.
Write unit tests with golden input-output pairs for each stage. Run these tests in CI so that changes to a stage prompt are validated immediately.
Complement stage-level unit tests with end-to-end tests on a representative sample of real inputs. Stage-level tests can pass while an inter-stage handoff is broken.

import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Stage 1 output schema: structured extraction
class ExtractedRequest(BaseModel):
    intent: str            # e.g., "refund", "status_check", "cancel"
    order_id: Optional[str]
    amount: Optional[float]
    urgency: str           # "low", "medium", "high"

# Stage 2 output schema: routing decision
class RoutingDecision(BaseModel):
    queue: str             # e.g., "standard", "priority", "finance"
    sla_hours: int
    requires_human: bool
    rationale: str

STAGE1_TOOL = {
    "name": "extract_request",
    "description": "Extract structured data from the customer message.",
    "input_schema": ExtractedRequest.model_json_schema(),
}

STAGE2_TOOL = {
    "name": "route_request",
    "description": "Decide how to route the extracted request.",
    "input_schema": RoutingDecision.model_json_schema(),
}

def stage1_extract(customer_message: str) -> ExtractedRequest:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",   # fast extraction model
        max_tokens=256,
        temperature=0,
        tools=[STAGE1_TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "any"},
        system="Extract intent, order_id, amount, and urgency from the customer message.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": customer_message}],
    )
    block = next(b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use")
    return ExtractedRequest.model_validate(block.input)

def stage2_route(extracted: ExtractedRequest) -> RoutingDecision:
    context = f"""
Extracted request:
- Intent: {extracted.intent}
- Order ID: {extracted.order_id or 'not provided'}
- Amount: {extracted.amount or 'not specified'}
- Urgency: {extracted.urgency}

Determine the routing queue (standard/priority/finance),
SLA in hours, whether human review is required, and your rationale.
"""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",   # reasoning model for routing logic
        max_tokens=256,
        temperature=0,
        tools=[STAGE2_TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "any"},
        system="You are a customer support routing agent.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": context}],
    )
    block = next(b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use")
    return RoutingDecision.model_validate(block.input)

# Pipeline runner
def process_customer_message(message: str) -> RoutingDecision:
    extracted = stage1_extract(message)
    routing = stage2_route(extracted)
    return routing

result = process_customer_message(
    "I urgently need a refund of $250 for order ORD-9876 "
    "placed last week. The item never arrived."
)
print(result.model_dump_json(indent=2))

import anthropic, json, logging
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional, Literal
from dataclasses import dataclass

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# ── Stage output schemas ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class DocumentSummary(BaseModel):
    title: str
    topic: str
    key_claims: list[str] = Field(max_length=5)
    document_type: Literal["research", "news", "opinion", "technical", "other"]
    word_count_estimate: int

class FactCheckResult(BaseModel):
    claims_checked: int
    verified_claims: list[str]
    unverified_claims: list[str]
    confidence: float  # 0.0–1.0
    notes: str

class FinalReport(BaseModel):
    executive_summary: str
    verified_claims: list[str]
    caveats: list[str]
    recommendation: Literal["publish", "review", "reject"]

# ── Stage implementations ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def make_tool(name: str, model: type[BaseModel], description: str) -> dict:
    return {
        "name": name,
        "description": description,
        "input_schema": model.model_json_schema(),
    }

def run_stage(stage_name: str, model_id: str, system: str,
              user_content: str, tool: dict, output_model: type) -> BaseModel:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model=model_id, max_tokens=1024, temperature=0,
        tools=[tool], tool_choice={"type": "any"},
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_content}],
    )
    block = next(b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use")
    result = output_model.model_validate(block.input)
    logger.info("Stage %s completed: %s", stage_name, json.dumps(block.input)[:200])
    return result

def stage1_summarise(document_text: str) -> DocumentSummary:
    return run_stage(
        "summarise", "claude-haiku-4-5",
        "Extract a structured summary from the document.",
        f"Document:\n{document_text[:4000]}",  # truncate to fit context
        make_tool("summarise", DocumentSummary, "Summarise the document."),
        DocumentSummary,
    )

def stage2_fact_check(summary: DocumentSummary) -> FactCheckResult:
    context = (
        f"Document type: {summary.document_type}\n"
        f"Key claims to check:\n" +
        "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in summary.key_claims)
    )
    return run_stage(
        "fact_check", "claude-opus-4-5",
        "Fact-check the listed claims based on your knowledge. Be conservative.",
        context,
        make_tool("fact_check", FactCheckResult, "Record fact-check results."),
        FactCheckResult,
    )

def stage3_report(summary: DocumentSummary, fact_check: FactCheckResult) -> FinalReport:
    context = (
        f"Topic: {summary.topic}\n"
        f"Verified: {fact_check.verified_claims}\n"
        f"Unverified: {fact_check.unverified_claims}\n"
        f"Fact-check confidence: {fact_check.confidence:.0%}\n"
        f"Notes: {fact_check.notes}"
    )
    return run_stage(
        "report", "claude-opus-4-5",
        "Write an executive summary and editorial recommendation.",
        context,
        make_tool("report", FinalReport, "Write the final editorial report."),
        FinalReport,
    )

# ── Pipeline runner ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

@dataclass
class PipelineResult:
    summary: DocumentSummary
    fact_check: FactCheckResult
    report: FinalReport

def editorial_pipeline(document_text: str) -> PipelineResult:
    summary = stage1_summarise(document_text)
    fact_check = stage2_fact_check(summary)
    report = stage3_report(summary, fact_check)
    return PipelineResult(summary=summary, fact_check=fact_check, report=report)

A legal firm processes 2,000 court documents per month. Each document needs to be extracted (party names, dates, case numbers), classified (motion type, jurisdiction, urgency level), and routed to the appropriate attorney team with a summary of key action items. Previously this was handled by paralegals — each document taking 20-45 minutes.

The sequential chain replaces paralegal pre-processing with three stages: Stage 1 (claude-haiku-4-5, extraction) runs in under 2 seconds and produces structured metadata; Stage 2 (claude-opus-4-5, classification) determines the document's urgency and appropriate team in under 3 seconds; Stage 3 (claude-opus-4-5, generation) produces a 200-word action summary for the assigned attorney. Total pipeline time: 6-8 seconds. Paralegal time is now reserved for the edge cases that the pipeline escalates — approximately 8% of documents — rather than the full 100%.
Stage 1 extraction is cached per document hash — re-classification for routing changes does not require re-extraction.
Intermediate outputs are stored in the firm's case management system, enabling attorneys to review the extraction and classification before acting on the summary.
The pipeline validates all three outputs against Pydantic schemas; validation failures at any stage are escalated to paralegals automatically.
Passing the Full Document to Every Stage

# WRONG: every stage receives the full raw document
def stage2_classify(raw_document: str, extracted: ExtractedRequest) -> RoutingDecision:
    # This stage doesn't need the raw document — just the extraction
    # Passing it inflates context, increases cost, and can distract the model
    context = f"Document: {raw_document}\nExtraction: {extracted.model_dump_json()}"
    ...

# RIGHT: selective handoff — Stage 2 only receives what Stage 1 produced
def stage2_classify(extracted: ExtractedRequest) -> RoutingDecision:
    context = f"Extracted data: {extracted.model_dump_json()}"
    ...

# WRONG: pass raw text between stages without validation
raw_stage1 = call_llm(stage1_prompt)
result = call_llm(stage2_prompt + raw_stage1)  # stage2 receives unvalidated text

# RIGHT: validate at every handoff
extracted = stage1_extract(input_text)   # returns Pydantic model or raises
routing = stage2_route(extracted)        # only runs if Stage 1 succeeded
report = stage3_report(extracted, routing)  # only runs if Stage 2 succeeded

A stage that can only be tested as part of the full pipeline is hard to iterate on and prone to regression. Write each stage as a pure function that takes typed inputs and returns a typed output, testable with a
parametrised test using golden input-output pairs. If the stage cannot be tested in isolation, it is probably not cleanly defined.

If the same large, expensive model is used for every stage, the cost savings of sequential chaining are not realised. Profile the requirements of each stage: extraction is typically a pattern-matching task where a smaller model performs well; generation requires a more capable model. Match model capability to task difficulty at each stage.
Define all inter-stage schemas before writing prompts:
The schemas are the specification. If you cannot write the schema for a stage's output, the stage is not well-defined.
Use Pydantic validation between every pair of adjacent stages. Never pass unvalidated text between stages.
Write a pytest parametrised test for each stage with at least three input-output pairs. Run these in CI.
Use the smallest model sufficient for each stage:
Extraction and classification typically need smaller models than generation. This reduces cost and often improves speed.
Store the validated output of each stage alongside the final output. This enables debugging without re-running the pipeline.
If Stage 1 extracts entities from a document, cache the result keyed by document hash. Re-runs of later stages for the same document do not need to re-invoke Stage 1.
Keep stage prompts short and focused:
A good stage prompt fits on one page. If the stage prompt is long and complex, the stage is probably doing too much and should be split.

Sequential chains have inherent latency multiplication: if each stage takes 500ms, a 4-stage chain has at least 2 seconds of latency. Optimise by profiling stage execution times and investing in the slowest stages first — usually generation stages using large models. For high-throughput pipelines, run multiple chain instances in parallel (each processing a different input document) rather than parallelising within a single chain.

Stage-level caching provides the most significant cost reduction for pipelines that process the same documents repeatedly with different generation prompts. Separate the extraction stage from the generation stage architecturally so the cache boundary is clean: extract once, generate many times.

For latency-sensitive applications, consider using streaming output from the final generation stage — the user sees the result beginning to appear as it is generated, reducing perceived latency even though the total time is the same.
operator for composing chain stages declaratively. Includes built-in retry and output parsing utilities.
Graph-based pipeline framework from LangChain. For sequential chains, use a simple linear graph with validated edges.
Workflow orchestration tools that add scheduling, monitoring, and retry logic to sequential pipelines. Useful when the pipeline is a scheduled batch job.
Adds automatic structured output and retry-on-validation-error to Anthropic and OpenAI calls. Makes stage implementation concise.
Traces multi-stage LLM pipelines, showing intermediate inputs and outputs for each stage. Essential for debugging inter-stage handoff failures.

Sequential chains reduce the attack surface for prompt injection relative to single-prompt systems. If Stage 1 extracts structured data from user input, the structure acts as a natural sanitisation layer: injected instructions embedded in the user's text become values in a Pydantic field (
), not free-form instructions that Stage 2 will interpret as directives. Stage 2's system prompt only references named fields — it never directly includes arbitrary user text.

For pipelines that perform irreversible operations, insert an explicit validation stage before the action stage. The validation stage checks that all preconditions are met and all required information is present and plausible. Only if the validation stage returns a clean result should the action stage be invoked. This pattern converts runtime failures (attempting an action with bad data) into pre-flight failures (detecting bad data before the action is attempted).

Think of a sequential prompt chain as an assembly line in a factory. Each station on the line performs exactly one operation — drill a hole, paint a panel, attach a part — and passes the product to the next station only if the operation succeeded. No station does more than one operation; no product skips a station; and a defect caught at Station 3 halts the line rather than allowing a defective product to reach Station 7. The assembly line metaphor also clarifies when not to use sequential chaining: if the product needs to be customised differently for each customer (branching), or if a part is sent back to an earlier station when an error is found (backtracking), a linear assembly line is the wrong model — a flexible workshop is needed instead.
A sequential chain is a fixed-order pipeline: Stage 1 produces output_1, Stage 2 receives output_1 and produces output_2, and so on — no model-decided branching.
Break complex tasks at boundaries where the output can be expressed as a complete, typed data structure — not at arbitrary text length thresholds.
Validate with Pydantic at every inter-stage handoff; a validation failure at Stage 2 is far cheaper to debug than a corrupted result at Stage 5.
Use the smallest model sufficient for each stage — extraction stages typically need smaller models than generation stages.
Log all intermediate stage outputs alongside the final result to enable post-hoc debugging without re-running the pipeline.
Test each stage independently with golden input-output pairs; a stage that can only be tested end-to-end is not cleanly specified.
Sequential chains reduce prompt injection risk because structured extraction in early stages acts as a natural sanitisation layer for user-supplied text.
When the execution order or number of steps must adapt to the content, use an agent loop — not a sequential chain.
